lessons.title: What is a Method
lessons.summary: Name and reuse blocks of code

contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you'll learn:

- What a [[method]] is (a reusable block of code)
- The syntax for defining and calling methods
- Why we use methods (code reusability)

By using methods, you can avoid writing the same code over and over.

contents.title: Defining and Calling Methods
contents.body: The syntax and basic usage of methods.
contents.note: The [[main method]] is also a method. Java program execution starts from the main method.

contents.title: Why Use Methods
contents.body:
Advantages of methods:

1. Reusability: Call the same logic as many times as you need
2. Readability: Named blocks of code are easier to understand
3. Maintainability: Only one place to make changes
4. Testability: Easier to test in small units

Method naming conventions:
| Item | Description |
|---|---|
| Start with a verb | calculateTotal, displayMenu, isValid |
| camelCase | getMaxValue (lowercase first letter) |
| boolean return type | Start with is, has, can |
  Example: isEmpty(), hasNext(), canExecute()

Return value rules:
- You can only return one value
- The return statement can be omitted in void methods
- When a return statement is executed, the method exits immediately
contents.note: If you want to return multiple values, use an [[array]], List, or a custom [[class]].

contents.title: Methods Are Like Cooking Recipes
contents.body:
A method is a "named set of instructions."

- Recipe name = method name
- Ingredients = arguments (parameters)
- Finished dish = return value

Once you write a recipe (method), you can call those instructions as many times as you like.
contents.note: In other languages, methods are also called "functions."

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- Method = a named, reusable block of code
- void: no return value, return statement can be omitted
- With a return value: specify the type and return a value with the return statement
- Method names start with a verb and use camelCase
- Benefits of methods: reusability / readability / maintainability / testability

quizzes.question: What is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: square(4) receives 4 as n and returns 4 * 4 = 16. The value returned by return is output by println.

quizzes.question: Which keyword is used when a method has no return value?
quizzes.explanation: The void keyword is used for methods that don't return a value. void means the method returns nothing. The return statement can be omitted, but if you want to exit the method early, you can write return;.
The return keyword sends a value back to the caller of the method. In this case, the result of Math.max(a, b) (the larger of the two arguments) is returned.

lessons.title: Arguments and Return Values in Detail

contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you'll learn:

- How to pass arguments (parameters)
- [[Methods]] with multiple arguments
- The difference between actual arguments and formal parameters

Let's understand how to pass data to methods.

contents.title: Pass by Value
contents.body: In Java, all arguments are passed **by value**. The behavior differs between [[primitive types]] and [[reference types]].
contents.note: Java does not have "pass by reference." Even for reference types, only "the value of the reference (a copy of the address)" is passed. Therefore, you can manipulate the object that the reference points to, but reassigning the reference itself does not affect the original [[variable]].

contents.title: Java Uses Pass by Value Only
contents.body:
Method arguments in Java always pass a "copy of the value."

| Argument Type | What Is Passed | Changes Inside the Method |
|---|---|---|
| Primitive (int, etc.) | Copy of the value | Does not affect the caller |
| Reference (array, object) | Copy of the reference | Object contents are changed |

Since a "copy of the reference" is passed, modifying the object's contents inside the method also affects the caller.

contents.title: Patterns for Returning Multiple Values
contents.body: A Java method can only return one value, but with some techniques you can return multiple values.
contents.note: With Java 14+ Records, you can define value-holding [[classes]] like Pattern 2 more concisely.

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- Java arguments are all **pass by value**
- Primitive types: a copy of the value is passed → no effect on the original
- Reference types: a copy of the reference is passed → object operations are reflected
- Reassigning the reference itself (= new ...) is not reflected
- Only one return value → use an [[array]] or class to return multiple values

quizzes.question: What is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: Since int n is a primitive type, a copy of the value is passed, and the original n remains unchanged (stays 10). Since int[] a is a reference type, arr[0] = 99 directly operates on the object, so a[0] changes to 99.

quizzes.question: Which description of Java's argument passing is correct?
quizzes.explanation: All arguments in Java are "pass by value." Primitive types pass a copy of the value itself, and reference types pass a copy of the reference (address). "Pass by reference" as in C++ does not exist in Java.

choices.text: Primitive types are pass by value, reference types are pass by reference
All are pass by value (reference types pass a copy of the reference value)
The developer can choose between pass by value and pass by reference

quizzes.question: What is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: String is also an Object (reference type), but s = "変更後" inside the method only replaces the copy of the reference with a new String. It does not affect the original text reference. Furthermore, String is immutable, so there is no way to modify the original string from inside the method.

lessons.title: Method Overloading
lessons.summary: Use same-named methods with different arguments

contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you'll learn:

- How to use return values (the return statement)
- The difference from void [[methods]]
- How to receive a method's result in a [[variable]]

Learn how to return calculated results from methods.

contents.title: Overloading Basics
contents.body: You can define methods with the same name as long as their "type," "number," or "order" of arguments differ.
contents.note: The criterion for **overloading** is the "parameter list (type, number, order)." The return type and [[access modifier]] are not relevant.

contents.title: The Relationship Between void Methods and return
contents.body:
There are methods that "return a value" and those that "don't."

| Return Type | Meaning | return Statement |
|---|---|---|
| int, String, etc. | Returns a value | return value; is required |
| void | Returns nothing | return; can be omitted |

Always be aware of "What does this method return?"

contents.title: Overloading and Type Conversion Priority
contents.note: On exams, questions about the priority between widening conversion and autoboxing are very common. Remember that widening conversion takes priority.

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- **Overloading**: distinguishing same-named methods by argument type, number, and order
- [[Overloading]] cannot be done by differing only in return type
- Type priority: exact match > widening > autoboxing > varargs
- println is a classic example of overloading

quizzes.question: Which of the following method definitions is valid as an overload? (Assume static int calc(int a) is already defined)
quizzes.explanation: Overloading requires a different number or type of arguments. static int calc(int a, int b) has a different number of arguments, so it is valid. static double calc(int a), which differs only in return type, causes a compilation error. Differing only in parameter name is also invalid.

choices.text: static double calc(int a) — differs only in return type
static int calc(int b) — differs only in parameter name
static int calc(int a, int b) — differs in number of parameters
int calc(int a) — differs only in access modifier

quizzes.question: Given the following methods, which one is called by test(10)?
quizzes.code:
static void test(long n)    { System.out.println("long"); }
static void test(Integer n) { System.out.println("Integer"); }
quizzes.explanation: When passing int value 10, there is no exact match, so widening conversion (int→long) takes priority. Since widening conversion has higher priority than autoboxing (int→Integer), test(long n) is called.

choices.text: test(long n) is called and outputs "long"
test(Integer n) is called and outputs "Integer"

quizzes.question: What is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: Since there are two arguments (String, int), greet(String name, int times) is called. The result is "Java x3". The overload is resolved by the number of arguments.

quizzes.question: Complete the method that returns the maximum of two integers.
quizzes.explanation: The method's return type is int, and it returns the larger of the two arguments with return. A common approach is to use Math.max() or a ternary operator.

contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you'll learn:

- **Overloading** of [[methods]] (methods with the same name but different arguments)
- How the compiler determines which method to call
- Rules and considerations for [[overloading]]

Learn how to use methods with the same name for different purposes.

contents.title: Varargs (Variable-Length Arguments)
contents.body: By adding ... after the type name, you can accept zero or more arguments.
contents.note: **Varargs** are internally converted to an [[array]] by the compiler. int... is the same as int[], but the caller doesn't need to explicitly create an array.

contents.title: Static Methods and Instance Methods
contents.body: [[Static methods]] belong to the [[class]], while [[instance]] methods belong to objects.
contents.note: The [[main method]] is static because no objects exist yet when the program starts.

contents.title: Conditions for Overloading
contents.body:
For **overloading** to work, the "parameter list must be different."

| OK/NG | Example | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| OK | add(int a) and add(int a, int b) | Different number of parameters |
| OK | add(int a) and add(double a) | Different parameter types |
| NG | int add(int a) and double add(int a) | Only return type differs |

Note that methods cannot be distinguished by return type alone.
contents.note: A method's "signature" is determined by "method name + parameter list."

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- **Varargs**: use type... to accept zero or more arguments
- [[Varargs]] must be the last parameter and only one per method
- Internally converted to an array
- Static methods: called with ClassName.methodName()
- Instance methods: called with object.methodName()
- Static methods cannot access instance [[variables]]

quizzes.question: What is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: The varargs String... words is internally treated as a String array. Since three arguments "a", "b", "c" are passed, words.length is 3.

quizzes.question: Which of the following code causes a compilation error?
quizzes.explanation: A compilation error occurs in m1. Varargs must be the last parameter in a method. In m1, String b comes after int... a, which is invalid. m2 and m3 have correct syntax.

quizzes.question: Which of the following correctly describes a characteristic of static methods?
quizzes.explanation: Static methods belong to the class, so they can be called without creating an object using ClassName.methodName(). However, they cannot directly access instance variables or instance methods. Math.max() and Integer.parseInt() are typical examples.

choices.text: Can be called without creating an object

quizzes.question: Select all correct conditions for method overloading.
quizzes.explanation: Overloading requires the same method name with a different parameter list (type, number, order). If only the return type differs, it causes a compilation error. Differences in access modifiers alone do not constitute overloading.

quizzes.question: Arrange the elements of a method definition in the correct order.
quizzes.explanation: A method definition follows the order: access modifier → static → return type → method name(parameters) → method body.

contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you'll learn:

- What [[variable]] [[scope]] (range of validity) is
- The lifetime of local variables
- How block scope works

Understanding where variables can be used helps prevent bugs.

contents.title: Variable Scope
contents.body: The range where a variable can be used is determined by blocks ({}).
contents.note: Local variables are not initialized with default values. You must assign a value before using them (otherwise it's a [[compilation]] error).

contents.title: Basic Rules of Scope
contents.body:
A variable's scope (range of validity) is limited to the block {} where it is declared.

- Declared inside a [[method]] → valid only within that method
- Declared in a [[for loop]] initializer → valid only within that for loop
- Declared inside an [[if statement]] block → valid only within that if block

Attempting to access a variable from outside its scope causes a compilation error.
contents.note: It's good design practice to keep variable scope as small as possible.

contents.title: Recursive Methods
contents.body: **Recursion** is a mechanism where a method calls itself.
contents.note: **Recursion** always requires a "base case (termination condition)." Without a base case, a [[StackOverflowError]] occurs. Since deep recursion consumes a lot of memory, loops are more suitable for large values.

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- Scope: [[class]] variable > local variable > block variable
- Local variables have no default values (must be initialized)
- **Recursion**: a method calls itself
- Recursion always requires a base case (termination condition)
- No base case → StackOverflowError
- Deep recursion consumes memory

quizzes.question: What is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: factorial(4) = 4 × factorial(3) = 4 × 3 × factorial(2) = 4 × 3 × 2 × factorial(1) = 4 × 3 × 2 × 1 = 24. The recursion terminates when n=1, reaching the base case.

quizzes.question: What happens when a recursive method has no base case (termination condition)?
quizzes.explanation: Without a base case, the method keeps calling itself infinitely, and the call stack (history of method calls) runs out of memory, causing a StackOverflowError. Since this is an Error class, it is not normally caught.

quizzes.question: Which line in the following code causes a compilation error?
quizzes.explanation: A compilation error occurs on line 6. The variable y is declared inside the if block, so its scope is limited to within the if block {}. It cannot be referenced from outside the block.

lessons.title: Method Design Best Practices
lessons.summary: Learn how to write good methods

contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you'll learn:

- How **recursion** (a [[method]] calling itself) works
- Termination conditions (base cases) for recursion
- Recursive solutions for factorials and the Fibonacci sequence

Recursion is a challenging concept, but it's a powerful technique for elegantly solving certain problems.

contents.title: Principles of Method Design
contents.body:
Characteristics of a well-designed method:

1. Single Responsibility: Each method should do only one thing
   ○ calculateTax(int price)
   × processOrderAndSendEmail()

2. Proper Naming: The method name alone should convey what it does
   ○ isAdult(int age)
   × check(int n)

3. Few Parameters: Ideally 0–2, maximum 3
   → If there are too many, bundle them into an object

4. Avoid Side Effects: Don't modify arguments or external state
   ○ int add(int a, int b) → just returns a computed result
   × void add(int[] arr, int val) → modifies the [[array]]

5. Early Return: Check error conditions first and return
   → Reduces nesting and improves readability
contents.note: The "Single Responsibility Principle" is fundamental to object-oriented design (SOLID principles). The same concept applies to [[class]] design.

contents.title: Good Methods vs. Bad Methods
contents.body: The same logic can vary greatly in readability depending on how it's written.
contents.note: Using early return (guard clauses) reduces method nesting and lets you focus on the happy path.

contents.title: The Pitfall of Recursion — Stack Overflow
contents.body:
A **recursive** method always needs a "termination condition (base case)."

Without a termination condition:
- The method keeps calling itself infinitely
- The call [[stack]] overflows, causing a [[StackOverflowError]]
- The program terminates abnormally

Steps for designing recursion:
1. Decide the **base case** (the condition that stops the recursion)
2. Write the **recursive case** (the part that calls itself)

It's important to design in this order.

contents.title: Splitting Methods
contents.body: An example of breaking a large method into smaller methods.
contents.note: Split methods are easier to unit test. You can independently test calcSum() and findMax().

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- Single responsibility: one method, one job
- Naming: start with a verb, name should describe what it does
- Keep parameters to 0–3. If more, bundle them into an object
- Early return: check error conditions first and return
- Minimize side effects: avoid modifying external state
- Split large methods into smaller ones

quizzes.question: What does the "Single Responsibility Principle" mean in method design?
quizzes.explanation: The Single Responsibility Principle means that each method (or class) should do only one job. This makes methods easier to understand, easier to test, and limits the impact of changes.

choices.text: A method should be written in one line
Each method should do only one job
A method should have only one parameter
A method should be called only once

quizzes.question: Which is the most appropriate method name? (A method that checks if a user is an adult)
quizzes.explanation: There is a naming convention to start boolean-returning methods with is, has, or can. isAdult(int age) clearly expresses "whether the person is an adult." check() is unclear about what it checks, and process() suggests processing rather than checking.

quizzes.question: The technique of checking error conditions at the beginning of a method and returning early is called a ____.
quizzes.explanation: A guard clause (early return) is a pattern where error conditions are checked at the beginning of a method and the method returns early. This significantly reduces nesting and improves readability.
